elements.sent-2411-118: A number of possible solutions then come to mind:.
elements.sent-2411-119: We can require that all names in a name vector must have the same type to be well-typed.
elements.sent-2411-120: Thus, if $x_1$ has type $T$ and $x_2$ has type $T$, then $x_1 \cdot x_2$ would have type $T$ as well, and so would $x_2 \cdot x_1$.
elements.sent-2411-121: This solution does not seem satisfactory, since it does not distinguish between using the names individually, and using the composition.
elements.sent-2411-122: We can let types be sets of capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-123: The type of a composition of names could then be the intersection of the sets.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, if \begin{equation}\Gamma \vdash x_1 : \SET{ \TCHAN{T_1}, \TCHAN{T_2} } \qquad \text{and} \qquad \Gamma \vdash x_2 : \SET{ \TCHAN{T_2}, \TCHAN{T_3} }\end{equation} then $\Gamma \vdash x_1 \cdot x_2 : \SET{ \TCHAN{T_2 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: However, that would lead to issues of non-well-foundedness, if we want to allow names to be sent on the vector, which are also in the vector.
elements.sent-2411-126: For example, to type the process $\OUTPUT{x_1 \cdot x_2 {x_1 .\NIL$, we would need the type of $x_1 \cdot x_2$ to contain $\TCHAN{T}$, which implies that both the types (sets) of $x_1$ and $x_2$ must contain $\TCHAN{T}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: But since $T$ is the type of $x_1$, then we have some non well-foundedness issues.
elements.sent-2411-128: We can allow names to appear in types to express which compositions should be allowed.
elements.sent-2411-129: This is the method used in the type system in \cite{huttel2011typed for the distributed \PI-calculus D\PI{ \cite{DPICALC , as described above in Section~\ref{wc:sec:introduction .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Suppose, for example, that we have $\Gamma \vdash x_1 : \SET{ x_2, x_3 }$, to signify that $x_1$ can be followed by either $x_2$ or $x_3$ in a composition.
elements.sent-2411-131: This avoids the problem of non-well-foundedness, since we could then have e.g.\@ that $\Gamma \vdash x_2 : \TCHAN{\SET{x_2, x_3 }$, which would make it possible to type the process $\OUTPUT{x_1 \cdot x_2 {x_1 .\NIL$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: However, having names appear directly in types creates other problems, if the names are bound.
elements.sent-2411-133: Consider the process \begin{equation}P \DEFSYM \NEW{x_2 : \TCHAN{\SET{x_2, x_3}}}( \OUTPUT{x_1 \cdot x_2}{x_1}.\NIL \PAR {y}.\NIL )\end{equation} with the type annotation added in the restriction.
elements.sent-2411-134: If $\Gamma \vdash x_1 : \SET{x_2, x_3}$ as before, then $P$ is well-typed.
elements.sent-2411-135: However, since $x_2$ is bound, then $P$ is \ALPHA-equivalent to another process $P'$ where the name $x_2$ has been converted to some other, fresh name, e.g.\@ $z$.
elements.sent-2411-136: But $z$ does not appear in the type of $x_1$ in $\Gamma$, so \ALPHA-conversion must also be applied to $\Gamma$, lest well-typedness would not be preserved by \ALPHA-equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-137: This is doable, but at least inelegant, since it would mean that all restrictions, at least implicitly, must be assumed to have scope over $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Furthermore, it also does not allow us to distinguish between the use of $x_2$ alone and in the composition $x_1 \cdot x_2$.
elements.sent-2411-139: In light of the considerations above, we shall choose our language of types as follows.
elements.sent-2411-140: Firstly, to avoid using channel names directly in types, we shall instead use named types as was done in \cite{carbone2005phd .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Thus, we introduce a separate, countably infinite set of atomic type names $\TNAMES$, ranged over by $I$, which is distinct from the set of channel names $\NAMES$.
elements.sent-2411-142: To avoid the issues of non-well-foundedness, we shall give every type a name from this set, i.e.\@ we shall be using named types.
elements.sent-2411-143: Secondly, the capability of a name to be used as a channel and its capability to be used in compositions appear to be more or less orthogonal.
elements.sent-2411-144: Hence, we shall distinguish between the `channel capabilities' of a name and its `composition capabilities.' We shall therefore use a pair for the type of a name: the first component describes whether the name can be used as a channel or not, and, in the former case, what it can be used to communicate; the second component describes whether the name can be composed with other names and, if so, which ones.
elements.sent-2411-145: Lastly, as $x_1 \cdot x_2$ is distinct from $x_2 \cdot x_1$, we may want to allow a name to have different meanings, depending on where it occurs in a composition.
elements.sent-2411-146: The type of a name is therefore not a single tuple, but a tree of capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-147: One way to view this is to think of each type name $I$ as a declaration of a name space, which in turn may contain other name space declarations.
elements.sent-2411-148: The idea in this type language is that a name $x$ should only have one base type $B$, as recorded in $\Gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-149: The type of a name should then almost always be a type name $I$, except when we type the continuation after an input, since the bound names here may also be substituted for the other data types.
elements.sent-2411-150: Furthermore, for a type name $I$ to be meaningful, it must also have its own entry in $\Gamma$, which must be a tuple $(C, \Delta)$, where $C$ is a capability type and $\Delta$ is a type environment that records the meaning of type names under the present name.
elements.sent-2411-151: We shall use the notation $I \mapsto (C, \Delta)$ to refer to a type name plus its entry, and we say a type environment $\Gamma$ is well-formed if all type names have entries.
elements.sent-2411-152: We shall only consider well-formed type environments in the following.
elements.sent-2411-153: We shall henceforth use a typed syntax for processes, with explicit (base) types added to the declaration of new names, i.e.\@ $\NEW{\VEC{x : \VEC{B P$, where $|\VEC{x | = |\VEC{B |$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Likewise, types must also now appear in the list of bound names in labels, so we write $\LSEND{\VEC{x {\NEW{\VEC{z : \VEC{B }\VEC{v }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The type language above is richer than the usual one for the \PI-calculus, although most of the complexities will not appear directly in the syntax of processes, since the idea is that names should only be given base types $B$.
elements.sent-2411-156: However, with this more complex type language, we can also achieve the same effect as some of the simpler solutions mentioned above, such as for example requiring that all names in a vector must have the same type.
elements.sent-2411-157: This can easily be achieved, since types are referred to by their type name $I$, so multiple names can have the same type (and not just structurally similar types).
elements.sent-2411-158: As an added benefit, named types also allow us to type processes such as $\OUTPUT{x {x .\NIL$ without requiring explicit recursive types to be added to the language, since $x$ can now simply be given the type $I \mapsto (\TCHAN{I , \EMPTYSET)$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-695: However, once we define an encoding, it is natural to also inquire about its quality, since there is by now a well-established literature on the topic (see~\cite{G12,G18,GORLA,gorla_nestmann2014_full_abstraction,Par08,PG15 , just to mention the most methodological papers).}
elements.sent-2411-696: We conjecture that the encoding presented in this paper satisfies most of the properties a `good encoding' should have, in particular operational correspondence and divergence sensitiveness.
elements.sent-2411-697: However, proving these properties for an encoding between languages that rely on different styles of semantics (small-step for \EPI{ vs.\@ big-step for \WCLANG) is a non-trivial task.}
elements.sent-2411-698: For example, the definition of when a statement diverges under a big-step operational semantics is in general not straightforward (see, e.g., \cite{Char13,LG09,PM14 on this topic).}
elements.sent-2411-699: We realise that for \WCLANG{ , divergence coincides with not having any (big-step) evolution; however, even with this simplifying feature, proving divergence reflection turned out to be technically challenging.}
elements.sent-2411-700: Since the quality of our encoding is an orthogonal issue to the main aim of this paper (that is the study of typing composite names), we prefer to leave this investigation for future work.

elements.sent-2411-707: As mentioned in Section~\ref{wc:sec:related_works , our work relates to that of Hüttel \cite{huttel2011typed, huttel2013resourcespsi, huttel2016sessionpsi , since these papers did not provide a general solution to the question of how to type composite subjects.}}
elements.sent-2411-708: It is therefore also worth emphasising here that our type system can be used to type the encoding of D\PI, even in the cases where the location name or the located subject name might be bound.
elements.sent-2411-709: This can be done by creating types for the locations $l$ of the form \begin{equation}I_l \mapsto (\TNIL, (I_{1} \mapsto (\TCHAN{B_1}, \EMPTYSET), \ldots, I_{n} \mapsto (\TCHAN{B_n}, \EMPTYSET)))\end{equation} and with the types $I_{i}$ assigned to the subjects $x_i$ occurring at location $l$.
elements.sent-2411-710: This closely corresponds to our encoding of the \WCLANG-types, with location names $l$ corresponding to class names $A$.
elements.sent-2411-711: The \WCLANG-language could in principle also have been encoded directly in D\PI, since the encoding only makes use of the $\PI^2$ fragment of \EPI.
elements.sent-2411-712: However, \WCLANG{ could easily be extended to allow nested class declarations, as is found in some object-oriented languages, i.e.\@ with declarations of the form \begin{equation}DC \DCLSYM \epsilon \ORSYM \code{class $A$ \{ $DC$ $DF$ $DM$ \}}\end{equation} which would then necessitate subject vectors of arbitrary length to encode an arbitrary nesting depth.}
elements.sent-2411-713: In the present paper, we have forgone this possibility for the sake of simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-714: Nevertheless, our correspondence results contribute to further the understanding of how a typed, object-oriented language may be represented in the setting of typed process calculi, whilst still preserving some of the structure afforded by interface types in such languages.
elements.sent-2411-715: A natural further step in this direction would be to consider subtyping, as was done for the \PI-calculus by Pierce and Sangiorgi \cite{PICAPABLE , who distinguish between the input and output capabilities of a channel.}
elements.sent-2411-716: Indeed, such a subtyping relation is also assumed (to be provided as a parameter) in the type systems by Hüttel for the \PSI-calculus, but none of the instance examples provide any hints as to how it might be defined.
elements.sent-2411-717: The type system for \EPI{ in \cite{carbone2005phd also does not have a subtyping relation, so the question of how it might be defined is entirely open.}}
elements.sent-2411-718: Input/output capabilities can of course easily be introduced for the channel capability component $C$ of our types, so the interesting question in this regard is rather what a subtyping relation might look like for the composition capability component $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-719: Again, the correspondence with object-oriented languages may provide some intuitions.
elements.sent-2411-720: For example, it would seem natural to require that the composition capabilities of the subtype $\Delta_1$ should be at least the same as the supertype $\Delta_2$; or that each type name $I$ in $\Delta_1$ again should be a subtype of an entry in $\Delta_2$.
elements.sent-2411-721: This would correspond to the usual understanding from object-oriented languages, where an object $O_1$ is a subtype of another object $O_2$, if $O_1$ contains at least the same public fields and methods as $O_2$, and the type of each such field, resp.\@ method, in $O_1$ again is a subtype of the type of the corresponding field, resp.\@ method, in $O_2$.. \bibliographystyle{eptcs.}
